_meta.source: EARS: The Easy Approach to Requirements Syntax, John Terzakis, Intel Corporation, ICCGI 2013 (originally Mavin et al., Rolls-Royce PLC, RE'09)
_meta.design_note: This rule set intentionally has NO overlapping rules. Each requirement gets exactly ONE pattern classification (EARS-P1 through EARS-P6 are mutually exclusive - only one should ever be the 'correct' pattern for a given requirement) plus independent checks against the ten orthogonal quality attributes (EARS-Q1 through EARS-Q10). Do not flag more than one pattern rule on the same requirement; instead identify which single pattern it SHOULD be and flag a mismatch if it uses the wrong one.

EARS-P1.description: A Ubiquitous requirement states a fundamental, always-true system property. It has no trigger, no precondition, and no state qualifier, and needs no pattern keyword. Format: 'The <system name> shall <system response>'.
trigger_criteria: Requirement is written in the bare 'shall' form but is NOT actually universal - it depends on an unstated trigger, state, or optional feature (this is the single most common misclassification; question every apparently-ubiquitous requirement before accepting it as such)
Requirement legitimately has no trigger/state/feature dependency but is written with an unnecessary pattern keyword (WHEN/IF/WHILE/WHERE) instead of the plain form
EARS-P1.example_violation: The system shall display the current vehicle speed. (this looks ubiquitous but almost certainly depends on an unstated state, e.g. ignition on, or trigger, e.g. a display refresh event)
EARS-P1.example_compliant: THE INSTRUMENT CLUSTER SHALL be certified to IEC 60950 for electrical safety.
EARS-P1.disambiguation: Before classifying a requirement as Ubiquitous, actively test it: does it require an event to execute, does it denote a state, or does it denote an optional feature? If yes to any, it is NOT ubiquitous and belongs to EARS-P2/P3/P4/P5 instead. Most requirements are NOT ubiquitous - treat this classification as the exception, not the default.

EARS-P2.description: An Event-Driven requirement is initiated when and only when a trigger occurs or is detected. Uses the 'WHEN' keyword. Format: 'WHEN <trigger> <optional precondition>, THE <system name> SHALL <system response>'.
trigger_criteria: Requirement describes a response to a discrete, expected event but does not use 'WHEN', or omits the trigger clause
'WHEN' is used but the underlying condition is actually a fault/error condition, which should instead be EARS-P3 ('IF...THEN')
EARS-P2.example_violation: The system shall load the driver profile for a detected key fob.
EARS-P2.example_compliant: WHEN a registered key fob is detected within range, THE BODY CONTROL MODULE SHALL load the associated driver seat and mirror profile within [500 ms].
EARS-P2.disambiguation: Reserve WHEN strictly for normal, expected, in-scope events. If the trigger is an undesired/abnormal condition (a fault, failure, or error), it belongs to EARS-P3, not here.

EARS-P3.description: An Unwanted Behavior requirement handles error conditions, failures, faults, disturbances, and other undesired events. Uses the 'IF' and 'THEN' keywords. Format: 'IF <unwanted condition or event>, THEN THE <system name> SHALL <system response>'.
trigger_criteria: Requirement describes handling of a fault/error/undesired condition but does not use the 'IF...THEN' construction
'IF...THEN' is used but the condition described is actually a normal, expected event, which should instead be EARS-P2 ('WHEN')
EARS-P3.example_violation: When the wheel speed sensor signal is lost, the system shall set a diagnostic trouble code.
EARS-P3.example_compliant: IF the wheel speed sensor signal is lost for more than [100 ms], THEN THE ABS MODULE SHALL set diagnostic trouble code C0035 within [50 ms].
EARS-P3.disambiguation: Reserve IF...THEN strictly for undesired/abnormal conditions: faults, failures, out-of-range values, disturbances. If the condition is a normal expected event, it belongs to EARS-P2, not here.

EARS-P4.description: A State-Driven requirement is triggered while the system is in a specific state. Uses the 'WHILE' keyword (or optionally 'DURING'). Format: 'WHILE <system state>, THE <system name> SHALL <system response>'.
trigger_criteria: Requirement's applicability depends on the system being in a persistent, ongoing state (a mode, a condition that holds true over an interval) but does not use 'WHILE'
'WHILE' is used to describe a discrete one-time event rather than a persisting state, which should instead be EARS-P2
EARS-P4.example_violation: In Park mode, the transmission control module disables the reverse gear signal.
EARS-P4.example_compliant: WHILE the transmission is in Park, THE TRANSMISSION CONTROL MODULE SHALL disable the reverse gear engagement signal.
EARS-P4.disambiguation: WHILE describes a condition that persists over an interval of time and can be checked as true/false at any moment (a mode or state). A one-time occurrence is an event and belongs to EARS-P2 or EARS-P3 instead.

EARS-P5.description: An Optional Feature requirement is invoked only in system configurations that include a particular optional feature. Uses the 'WHERE' keyword. Format: 'WHERE <feature is included>, THE <system name> SHALL <system response>'.
trigger_criteria: Requirement's applicability depends on an optional, build-time configuration item (e.g. a trim-level option, an installed hardware module) but does not use 'WHERE'
'WHERE' is used to describe a runtime operating condition rather than a configuration-time option, which should instead be EARS-P4
EARS-P5.example_violation: For vehicles equipped with a heads-up display, the software shall project the current speed onto the windshield.
EARS-P5.example_compliant: WHERE the Heads-Up Display feature is installed, THE INFOTAINMENT SYSTEM SHALL project the current vehicle speed onto the windshield.
EARS-P5.disambiguation: WHERE identifies a build/configuration-time option present or absent for a given vehicle. A condition that changes during vehicle operation (a mode) is a state and belongs to EARS-P4 instead.

EARS-P6.description: A Complex requirement describes conditional events involving multiple triggers, states, and/or optional features together, using a combination of WHEN, IF/THEN, WHILE, and WHERE.
trigger_criteria: Requirement legitimately combines two or more condition types (state + trigger, feature + trigger, etc.) but the clauses are run together as unstructured prose rather than clearly separated by their correct keywords
A single-condition requirement is unnecessarily forced into a multi-keyword complex form when one of EARS-P2 through EARS-P5 alone would suffice
EARS-P6.example_violation: If the vehicle has adaptive cruise control and is moving above 30 kph and the lead vehicle brakes hard, the system should slow down.
EARS-P6.example_compliant: WHERE the Adaptive Cruise Control feature is installed, WHILE the vehicle speed is above [30 kph], WHEN the lead vehicle deceleration exceeds [0.3 g], THE ACC MODULE SHALL apply the service brakes within [200 ms].
EARS-P6.disambiguation: Only classify as Complex when two or more genuinely distinct condition types co-occur. If a single condition type suffices, use the simpler EARS-P2/P3/P4/P5 pattern instead of forcing a hybrid structure.

EARS-Q1.description: A requirement is complete when it contains sufficient detail to guide the work of those who use it, leaving no gap that forces a designer or developer to guess.
trigger_criteria: Requirement contains a placeholder, TBD, or an undefined parameter that a reader would have to guess or invent
Requirement omits a necessary detail (a threshold, a count, a condition) that is needed for someone to implement or test it
EARS-Q1.example_violation: The system shall allow a TBD number of failed start attempts before locking out the ignition.
EARS-Q1.example_compliant: WHEN more than [3] failed start attempts occur within [30 s], THE IMMOBILIZER MODULE SHALL lock out the ignition for [5 minutes].
EARS-Q1.disambiguation: Independent of EARS pattern classification - a requirement can use the correct pattern keyword and still be incomplete if it leaves a required detail unspecified.

EARS-Q2.description: A requirement is correct when it is error-free and matches the actual system behavior or constraint intended, as verifiable against source materials or subject matter experts.
trigger_criteria: Requirement contains a factual, numerical, or logical error that conflicts with known source material, specifications, or domain constraints
EARS-Q2.example_violation: THE FUEL GAUGE SHALL indicate a range of 0 to 150 liters. (the tank capacity per the vehicle spec is 60 liters)
EARS-Q2.example_compliant: THE FUEL GAUGE SHALL indicate a range of 0 to 60 liters, consistent with the vehicle fuel tank capacity specification.
EARS-Q2.disambiguation: Correctness is a factual-accuracy check against source truth, distinct from ambiguity (EARS-Q7) or internal consistency (EARS-Q9).

EARS-Q3.description: A requirement is concise when it contains just the needed information expressed in as few words as possible, free of compound statements, embedded rationale, or overly complex grammar.
trigger_criteria: Requirement bundles multiple distinct requirements into one compound statement
Requirement embeds rationale, background, or design justification inline instead of expressing only the requirement itself
Requirement uses unnecessarily complex or wordy grammar that could be simplified without losing meaning
EARS-Q3.example_violation: The advanced infotainment software, developed by the multimedia team to improve the driving experience, shall display the current outside temperature when the well-designed climate menu is opened by the driver.
EARS-Q3.example_compliant: WHEN the driver opens the climate menu, THE INFOTAINMENT SYSTEM SHALL display the current outside temperature.
EARS-Q3.disambiguation: Distinct from EARS-P6 (Complex pattern), which concerns legitimate multiple condition types; this rule concerns unnecessary wordiness and bundling regardless of pattern type.

EARS-Q4.description: A requirement is feasible if at least one design and implementation exists (or can reasonably be shown, e.g. via prototyping or analysis) to satisfy it.
trigger_criteria: Requirement demands a capability with no known or plausible implementation path (e.g. an unbounded resource, a physically unachievable tolerance)
EARS-Q4.example_violation: THE TELEMATICS MODULE SHALL support an unlimited number of simultaneously connected Bluetooth devices.
EARS-Q4.example_compliant: THE TELEMATICS MODULE SHALL support up to [5] simultaneously connected Bluetooth devices.
EARS-Q4.disambiguation: Feasibility concerns whether the requirement can physically/technically be implemented at all, distinct from EARS-Q1 (completeness of the stated detail) and EARS-Q10 (realistic absolute claims are also covered under this rule when infeasibility stems from an unbounded/absolute claim).

EARS-Q5.description: A requirement is necessary when it is market-competitive, traceable to a stakeholder need, establishes a genuine differentiator, or is dictated by business/regulatory strategy - not included merely as an unbounded or speculative goal.
trigger_criteria: Requirement's scope goes beyond any identifiable stakeholder need, regulation, or business driver (e.g. supporting compatibility with every possible past variant rather than a defined, current set)
EARS-Q5.example_violation: THE DIAGNOSTIC TOOL SHALL be compatible with every diagnostic protocol ever used by the company.
EARS-Q5.example_compliant: THE DIAGNOSTIC TOOL SHALL be compatible with UDS (ISO 14229) and OBD-II (SAE J1979) protocols.
EARS-Q5.disambiguation: This is a scope-justification check, distinct from feasibility (EARS-Q4); a requirement can be technically feasible yet still unnecessary because its scope isn't tied to any real need.

EARS-Q6.description: A requirement is prioritized when it is ranked or ordered according to its relative importance, since all requirements compete for limited engineering resources.
trigger_criteria: Requirement record has no priority classification (e.g. High/Medium/Low, or Essential/Desirable/Nice-to-have) associated with it in the requirement set metadata
Requirement set treats all requirements as equally critical with no differentiation
EARS-Q6.example_violation: Requirement set has no Priority field, or every requirement is marked 'Critical'.
EARS-Q6.example_compliant: SYS_REQ_0045 is tagged Priority: High (safety-related); SYS_REQ_0046 is tagged Priority: Low (convenience feature).
EARS-Q6.disambiguation: This is a requirement-set/metadata check, not a wording check on the statement text itself.

EARS-Q7.description: A requirement is unambiguous when it possesses a single interpretation, independent of the reader's background.
trigger_criteria: Requirement uses a subjective or vague qualifier (e.g. 'quickly', 'user-friendly', 'as needed') that different readers could interpret differently
Requirement lacks a measurable/verifiable criterion where multiple readings are otherwise possible
EARS-Q7.example_violation: THE PARKING ASSIST SYSTEM SHALL respond quickly to detected obstacles.
EARS-Q7.example_compliant: WHEN an obstacle is detected within [1 m], THE PARKING ASSIST SYSTEM SHALL apply the brakes within [200 ms] in [95%] of test cycles.
EARS-Q7.disambiguation: Distinct from EARS-Q1 (completeness of detail) - a requirement can already list every needed field yet still use a subjective term that different readers would interpret differently.

EARS-Q8.description: A requirement is verifiable if it can be proved that it was correctly implemented, via demonstration, analysis, inspection, or testing.
trigger_criteria: Requirement describes an outcome with no defined method or measurable criterion by which correct implementation could be demonstrated, analyzed, inspected, or tested
EARS-Q8.example_violation: THE SEAT MEMORY FEATURE SHALL be easy to configure for the driver.
EARS-Q8.example_compliant: THE SEAT MEMORY FEATURE SHALL save the current seat position to one of [3] memory slots within [2 s] of the driver pressing and holding the SET button.
EARS-Q8.disambiguation: Overlaps in spirit with EARS-Q7 (unambiguous) but is specifically about the existence of a verification method, not just interpretive clarity; a requirement can be unambiguous in meaning yet still lack any defined way to verify it was met.

EARS-Q9.description: A requirement is consistent when it does not conflict with any other requirement at any level of the requirement set.
trigger_criteria: Requirement directly contradicts another requirement in the set (e.g. one requirement restricts an input format that another requirement requires)
EARS-Q9.example_violation: SYS_REQ_0010: the system shall only accept whole-number odometer entries. SYS_REQ_0011: the system shall accept odometer entries in tenths of a mile.
EARS-Q9.example_compliant: SYS_REQ_0010: the system shall only accept whole-number odometer entries, except when the tenths-of-a-mile entry mode (SYS_REQ_0011) is selected.
EARS-Q9.disambiguation: Cross-requirement check requiring comparison against the full requirement set, not evaluable from a single requirement in isolation; distinct from EARS-Q2 (correctness against source truth).

EARS-Q10.description: A requirement is traceable when it is uniquely and persistently identified with a tag, enabling change-impact assessment, coverage analysis, and scope management across the requirement set.
trigger_criteria: Requirement has no unique, persistent identifier (ID or name) associated with it
EARS-Q10.example_violation: The system shall prompt the driver for a PIN before enabling valet mode. (no requirement ID present)
EARS-Q10.example_compliant: SYS_REQ_VALET_003: THE SYSTEM SHALL prompt the driver for a PIN before enabling valet mode.
EARS-Q10.disambiguation: This is a metadata/identification check, independent of the wording quality of the requirement statement itself.
